question: Explain the difference between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling in cloud environments. Provide one advantage of each.
expectedPoints: Vertical scaling adds resources to existing instance (CPU/RAM/storage)
Horizontal scaling adds more instances to handle load
Vertical advantage: Simple, no architectural changes needed
Horizontal advantage: Better fault tolerance, unlimited scalability

question: What is Auto Scaling? Describe any two benefits of implementing auto scaling in cloud infrastructure.
expectedPoints: Auto Scaling automatically adjusts compute resources based on demand
Benefit 1: Cost optimization - only pay for resources needed
Benefit 2: Improved availability - maintains desired instance count
Benefit 3: Elasticity - handles traffic variations automatically

question: Define Right-sizing in cloud capacity planning. How does it help in cost optimization?
expectedPoints: Right-sizing matches instance capacity to actual resource requirements
Prevents over-provisioning (wasting money)
Prevents under-provisioning (performance issues)
Involves analyzing utilization metrics and adjusting instance types/sizes

question: Explain the concept of Reserved Instances in cloud computing. When would you recommend using Reserved Instances?
expectedPoints: Reserved Instances offer discounts (up to 60%) for committed usage
Requires 1-3 year commitment
Recommended for predictable, steady-state workloads
Useful when baseline capacity is known and consistent

question: What is a Load Balancer? List two benefits of using Load Balancers in cloud architecture.
expectedPoints: Load Balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple instances
Benefit 1: Fault tolerance - routes traffic to healthy instances only
Benefit 2: Improved performance - distributes load evenly
Benefit 3: Scalability - enables horizontal scaling

question: Describe the factors that should be considered while planning cloud capacity for an organization.
expectedPoints: Current and projected workload patterns
Performance requirements (latency, throughput)
Budget constraints and cost optimization strategies
Scalability requirements and growth projections
High availability and disaster recovery needs

question: What are Spot Instances? Discuss their advantages and limitations for production workloads.
expectedPoints: Spot Instances are unused cloud capacity available at steep discounts (up to 90%)
Advantages: Cost savings for fault-tolerant workloads
Limitations: Can be interrupted with short notice (2 minutes)
Not suitable for critical production workloads without backup strategies

question: Discuss the cloud capacity planning process in detail. Explain how you would develop a capacity planning strategy for an enterprise application migrating to cloud.
expectedPoints: Understanding current and future workload requirements
Analyzing application architecture and dependencies
Determining baseline and peak capacity needs
Selecting appropriate scaling strategies (vertical/horizontal/auto)
Choosing right pricing models (on-demand, reserved, spot)
Implementing monitoring and optimization processes
Planning for scalability and growth
Cost-benefit analysis and optimization

question: Explain the working of Auto Scaling in cloud environments. Discuss the components of auto scaling and how scaling policies are defined.
expectedPoints: Auto Scaling Group (ASG): Collection of instances with similar characteristics
Launch Configuration: Defines instance type, AMI, security groups
Scaling Policies: Define when and how to scale (metric-based, scheduled, predictive)
Health Checks: Monitor instance health for replacement
Cool-down Period: Prevents rapid scaling oscillations
Metrics: CPU, memory, network, custom application metrics

question: Design a cloud capacity strategy for an e-commerce application that experiences significant traffic variation during festival sales. Include scaling approaches, cost optimization techniques, and high availability considerations.
expectedPoints: Predictive auto scaling based on historical sale data
Horizontal scaling for handling traffic spikes
Reserved instances for baseline capacity
Spot instances for non-critical batch processing
Multi-AZ deployment for high availability
Load balancing for traffic distribution
Cache and CDN for performance optimization
Cost monitoring and right-sizing regularly

question: Compare and contrast different cloud pricing models (On-Demand, Reserved Instances, Spot Instances). For each model, suggest appropriate use cases.
expectedPoints: On-Demand: Pay per use, no commitment - for unpredictable workloads, testing/development
Reserved: Discounted rate with commitment - for predictable baseline workloads
Spot: Cheapest but interruptible - for fault-tolerant batch jobs, stateless applications
Savings Plans: Flexible commitment - for consistent usage with variable needs
Use case selection depends on workload predictability, criticality, and budget
